speaker: interviewer
text: --that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult until I learned the English language, but I was lucky because I loved to read. And my-- my father-- I saw a father who was who-- who I knew was very learned, very orthodox, and he-- the things of what I saw-- [? shabbos ?] celebrated, real orthodox. Everything around me, you know, was very orthodox. So I-- it wasn't hard to become what I-- what I was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have the same feeling, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it slowly-- it had to slowly-- but let me explain you something. Were it not for this terrible Holocaust, I think I would have had a little bit more faith. I have to admit this right here, now. And I'm not ashamed to say this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, every day of my existence I ask, why-- why did this happen? Why did this have to happen? And why me? Why was I chosen to survive and not my family? And I ask this, and no answers are coming. So, for that reason-- for that reason, I think I would have been-- I think my faith would have been stronger. Because, you know, when you go through a normal process of believing, you-- in other words, some people maybe have more faith in spite of it, but I was left with-- I believe I would have been more genuine. More [? genuine ?] than I am, even though I have to say I am orthodox. I try to fulfill the basic [? mitzvahs-- ?] what a woman's supposed to-- supposed to fulfill as an orthodox woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think, deep down in me, I have questions, and I don't care who's-- who the person is who thinks they're holier than thou, like this expression. I don't care. I don't-- I-- I-- I-- I'm not afraid of this, because I will tell you one thing. I read the Hasidic tales from the Bluzhever Rebbe, and he himself was the rabbi from the dynasty that my [INAUDIBLE] followed. And he himself said he doesn't know how a person can still have the faith after going through because he-- through all the Holocaust. And he himself was in Belzec, which very few people survived. And I want to bring out-- he was a witness to what could happen to a rabbi. He was a rabbi before the war. And if he could say, so I'm not afraid to say that I don't understand why this happened. I question, and I think I would have had more faith were it not-- were it not for what happened to me. I think I would have had more faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I do observe. I am an observant person. I do believe in God, and I believe that God must have had a plan for why he did-- why this happened and I am too stupid to understand. I'm not smart enough to understand. So, but I do-- I still would like-- I mean, many times in my mind I wonder-- I ask why. Well, why this-- why this terrible catastrophe happened to such good people? Did you ever make any special friends while you were going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were. Yes, there were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Americans who had never been through--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. No. They were-- they were, like-- or so-- that came after the war. But even that, I think, was-- I think it affected me, even making friends. I think it affected me. My life affected me. You know, it's easy to be a normal child and have normal skills. But because I went through what I did, I think even that part was spoiled for me. You see, you must-- you need-- in every way in your life, you need to have deep trust for everything. It affects all your trust. You see, trust permeates into all parts of your life, whether it's friends, or-- or you know, whether it's living-- like, I'll give you an example. If I walk on the street, I will turn around very often, even today. I think the fright in me is still a little bit. It's hard to believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm always-- I'm not afraid. I will go out sometimes when it gets dark, but I'm always aware-- very aware. I'm always on check, always on guard, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this comes from-- I know this was left from the war, because one time I was walking in the middle of the war, and the man-- this Jewish, Polish man who I told you this-- this sister I met. And he-- he-- was-- his house was on such where I had to go to his house-- I had to walk, like, a block towards his house. And there was no houses in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was always turning around. He says, why do you walk and you're always turning around? And I realized how afraid I was of my own shadow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- I don't-- some part of this was left in me. You see, that fright in me was left till today, 50 years later. This part of the psyche. It was-- I can never be, like-- for instance, I'll give you an example.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I walk and I'm in a bus, and some people are talking loud Jewish, I will not talk loud. I'm still-- I sort of-- I don't feel comfortable because I felt like a survivor of being a Jewish woman. You understand what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, there is a certain amount of fright or whatever was left from the war. And this never leaves me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it affect your friendships?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affected because the trust-- the trust wasn't as-- you know, the skills of making friends, the skills. It was-- Everything was affected-- affected me. And I would say it affected me. Now when I look, I understand myself better. I was able to think about many things. I'm at an age where I have plenty of time to think. And I realized that all these things affected me because of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, a lot of skills that children have in a normal life you don't realize. I never-- I never-- and then you suppresses it. In other words, if you-- if you lose certain skills later, you suppress it. You just-- you don't bother with certain feelings, you just deal with them, you know, privately. And you just, you know, try to function as best as you can. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as catching up with reading, yes, that I do because I enjoy it. It doesn't interfere as far as-- because I can do that, you know, with myself. Nobody has to do that. I can read and I-- I-- I grew a lot because of the reading I do, and I learned a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- in that area, I think it didn't affect me because I had a great liking for reading and this. So, in that area, I'm OK. It's just that many skills, I think I lost as a result, or didn't acquire, and then later I didn't bother. I didn't bother to-- to-- to develop. You understand what I'm saying? Did you go to any social events--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --like more concerts or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that many. I went, yes, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I had cousins. My mother had cousins-- many, here in America. And one of these cousins was married to Mr. Barouch. [? Barouch, ?] you say in English. And he was from the town where my mother was born. He was the same amount of generations that my mother's family lived in that shtetl. His family lived in the shtetl. And besides, he was married to my mother's first cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew my husband from business, and me, he knew because I was a cousin to his wife. And he knew my family going back. My family knew his family, all the way. So he knew my background, you know? And that's how he introduced my husband to me, and that's how I met my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very young. And I was-- I remember I came back from camp, from Bais Yaakov camp, and that's when he called me. And we went out, and then we married seven months later. And we had two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Pinchas, the older one. My daughter's name is Gloria. And they have children. I have grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Rachelli ?] and [? Elie, ?] his boy. And he is a lovely young man. He's growing into a very fine young man. I'm very proud of him. And then there are twin boys. Their name is David and Jonathan. And they are lovely boys. Good, handsome, boys, and I'm very-- I realize maybe that was the reason why-- I don't know. I'm just saying, if that is, maybe it's enough. But I say something I left-- if I die, I will leave something. In other words, some, you know, Jewish people to follow me. So that's something. I think it's something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel that, as a-- that being a survivor-- and for you, as a survivor who went through this as a child and, like you said, lost her childhood-- did not get a chance to experience it. How do you feel that affected the way you raised your own children?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affect a great deal. I didn't have the skills what a normal person had-- learns from their parents. You have to be taught. Even animals, you know, to be taught how to do things. Even an animal has to be taught, shown by example. Birds-- everybody of their own kind, they have to be shown how to do everything. A child learns from their own parent, everything, just by watching. And I didn't have that. And I missed that, and I did the best I could. I didn't have the skills what some people have, but I think I did the best that was-- that was my-- in my skill, that I knew how to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that while you were raising-- especially with the daughter-- while you were raising your daughter that you would think back about your mother and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. But I remember one time I mentioned something to my daughter when she was married. And I remember my mother was married later because she was engaged to a cousin, and the marriage-- I mean, the engagement fell apart, and she married because of that later. And then when my daughter got married very close, I said to her-- and she got-- and she got very angry at me. She says to me, do you mean that I should-- in other words, she didn't take it the right way.

speaker: interviewer
text: She thought that I want her to be wished dead because, in other words, my mother was married late, OK? My daughter was married later, too. But I don't think children always understand what their parents want to convey to them. I think sometimes there's-- there's a misunderstanding. So, you know, I tried the best that I could. I tried with what I had to work with, with what I was able to give. You have to realize that my parents-- I lost my mother at 9 and 1/2. Now, how much skills could I have had? My stepmother didn't give me that much, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married only four years after I came to America, I was, you know, and I was barely a-- you know, it was just four years, and then I was married. So-- so I didn't really have that many skills. Nobody taught me how to be a-- I didn't see my mother being a mother to me. In other words, from 9 and 1/2, I lost her. So-- so, this is what I'm trying to say-- that the skills, I didn't have. And today, I feel sorry that I didn't have the skills, but there was nothing I could do. I did the best I could. That's what I'm feeling. I did the best I knew how. You know, I don't believe that a mother has any bad-- in other words, a mother-- I think most mothers-- I'm not saying every mother, because some mothers-- I see [? in the ?] news and the paper that they do harm their-- but I believe that most mothers mean the best for their children. That's what my feeling is-- most. They have a genuine feeling that the children should have-- you know, wish that the children should turn out good. And some mothers don't have-- like, I did not have the skills because I didn't have where to get them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your husband a survivor, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he is. He survived in another part of the world-- in China, in Japan. And he went through his own Holocaust years. But he didn't have it as bad because he sat and learned. He benefited in one way. He was able to sit and learn and get something out of it. I lost everything-- my-- my education, because I wasn't able to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wasn't able to have the proper-- he had some food. He wouldn't have to scrounge for food like I. I had to scrounge for food every day, if I got it. And I-- and I had to scrounge for where to sleep. Where am I going to sleep every night? He didn't have to do that, so he had it easier. He had a bed where to sleep every night. I did not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I every night, I had the nightmares at night. In the daytime, I had the nightmare that somebody's going to kill me-- going to see me and they're going to kill me. Every day during the war years. And it affected my whole, entire life. Till today, many things remind me-- normal things. What most people wouldn't think of remind me of something connecting to when I was hiding. You wouldn't believe the things they remind me of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like snow. Like, every time snow comes, deep snow, I remember the snow was higher in Poland in the war and I had no shoes and no clothes to put on. And I was totally alone in the dark, walking from village to village. Now, how can this not remind you of-- of-- of-- of snow, when the snow comes?

speaker: interviewer
text: And whenever there comes a hurricane, I say, what is this? Big deal. I had to go-- I mean, I had no clothes. And then people laugh at me. I put on my boots today, I put my warm clothes, and I walk to Main Street. My daughter-- there was once a hurricane and they were closed in. They thought they could not get out. To me, this is nothing. This was normal in Poland, and I had no clothes. So I laugh at everybody. The other day I walked out. It was very cold. She says, oh, it's so cold. Bitter cold. I said, what? This is bitter cold? You call this bitter cold? You would know what bitter cold is if you had to experience what I went through. Then you would know what bitter cold this. This is one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then another thing, when you eat corn. A person-- when normal, you eat corn. You enjoy corn. When I eat corn, it reminds me of hiding among the corn when I saw, my last time, my grandmother. And I never saw her again. So corn brings me memories of hiding, and snow brings me the memory of cold and being frozen-- frozen, and no clothes. And so many other things-- a piece of bread. I mean normally-- I remember, if I got a piece of bread with butter, that was a big thing in the war, and most people take it for granted. You know, they take it for granted. You know, normal. But to me, many things remind me. Many things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today, do you have any friends that have never gone through the Holocaust that you ever speak to about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think anybody can understand. I don't think a normal person that lived a normal life-- and you know what? I will tell you something. I think most people don't want to hear it. They turn off, most people. I met so many people. And it's like Daisy Miller-- she said a very good thing. She was a Holocaust survivor, but she never went through what I did. She went through-- she was in the west, and she survived-- a hidden child. And she said something which I think is very appropriate. I will quote her. She lives in Los Angeles and she works for the Shoah, I think. Many people know her, Daisy Miller, a very beautiful-looking lady. And when I was at the gathering, she said something which I cannot say it better. Whenever she tried to express her inner pains of what she went through when she tried, sometimes people took it so lightly they would say, please pass the butter. That's all it meant-- please pass the butter. Like as if that's all it meant. Is-- They took it so lightly, like it meant, like, passing the butter or the bread. That's what it meant. And that's how I feel that most people, no matter how-- after a certain point, they don't want to hear too much. They want to hear, but they don't want to hear too much because it hurts too much, and they don't want to hear too much. They cannot take too much. I think it's very important for all the people that went through to talk about it and express their pain. I think it helps to get it out, and I think it's like unloading a load off your soul. I think it's like carrying a heavy load and unloading it. That's the way I feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had one message that you could leave for your children, your grandchildren, and all future generations, what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: My one message is that the people who died in the Holocaust-- my family and the other Jewish people-- they should never be forgotten. This is my message. They should never be forgotten. It should be even mentioned in the Haggadah. When [? there ?] [? is ?] mentioned the things what [INAUDIBLE] went through, what they experienced, I believe this wasn't [INAUDIBLE] that none of them [? made. ?] But I think it should be mentioned in the Haggadah. That it should be read through, or one [? book ?] should be mentioned once a year, that the Jewish people, that they were not forgotten-- the ones that didn't make it. This is my message. They should never be forgotten, just like the Haggadah is being read every-- [SOBBING] I can't. The Haggadah is being read because it should never be forgotten. [? And ?] here, I think it should be mentioned they should never be forgotten. That the Jewish people-- millions died. Children, grandparents, mothers, fathers-- they should never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Baba [INAUDIBLE], and her maiden name was [? Bilah ?] Zucherman, and she married Aziz [INAUDIBLE], my grandfather-- my great grandfather, excuse me, my great grandfather. Now, she was born in Belgrade, and she lived-- she lived-- after the First World War, it was hard times, and her husband died because there was disease raging, cholera. And he buried our cousin and the next day, he died. And this is the Baba [INAUDIBLE]. She died a normal death. She had this [NON-ENGLISH] to die a normal death without the Germans touching her. And some of her grandchildren-- some of her great grandchildren managed to come to America, and live a normal life. And they are all orthodox. Most of her great grandchildren-- I was one of the only great grandchild, I am, that survived the war, that was born in Europe. There was no other great grandchild that survived the war of hers that was born in Europe. The other great grandchildren were born in America. And she had great [NON-ENGLISH], because she was buried in a grave, but there is no marker to where she was buried because the Germans destroyed the cemeteries. But she was buried, a normal death. The picture is of Gittel. She was born Esther Suswein in this-- in the-- in the shtetl of Dubiecko, near Dynow. And her parents were Jossel and Esther Suswein. And she was the oldest of eight children. And she did not survive, and neither did most of her-- six of her brothers and sisters were killed in the Holocaust. Only two brothers survived. And she was-- and she was-- I believe she was killed in Belzec, in the death camp. And I don't know even where her ashes are. She was a beautiful lady. She was orthodox and she lived according to Jewish law, but she died a terrible death. This picture is of Chava. Atlas was her married name. She was the only one that married from my-- was married-- of my mother's sisters or brothers. But her maiden name was Suswein, Havah Suswein. And she was born in Dubiecko, near Dynow. And she died in the-- she was killed in the Holocaust. This picture's of Rahel Suswein. And she never had a chance to get even married before the Second World War. And the Nazis killed her. She was born in Dubiecko, the shtetl of Dubiecko, near Dynow.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's closer to you that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is Ephraim [? Nuham ?] Einsidler. He was born in the shtetl Grodzisko Dolne, near Lezhinsk, or Lizhensk. And he managed to come before the war, to go out from Poland, and reach America eventually. And he lived in America, and he lived here many years. And he lived in Israel, and he's buried in Yerushalayim. The little girl sitting on the chair is Raizel Einsiedler. And she was a tiny, little girl here, maybe 2. And on the-- on-- standing is [? Fridella ?] Einsiedler. And they are sisters, of course. And only one sister is missing there. And it was before the Second World War-- normal times. It was taken next to my house, in sort of like a-- not a real garden, but sort of like a garden. And my father already left, so we were without our father. But my mother, you can see, dressed us nice. And these were handmade clothes. They were made by hand. And it was in the summer, somewhere between, maybe, '38 or-- 1938, I believe. This postcard was sent by my mother, [? Getal ?] Einsiedler, to her aunt, Rose Gold, who lived in America. This was her father's sister. And she was asking for help. And I guess the postcard was the quickest way you could send a message. And somehow, this card arrived among others asking for help. The time was 1941 in Brzeziny, and times were very difficult. It was in the war. And my mother didn't have-- she had something to give us to eat, but not enough. She didn't have all the things that we were used to as normal children. And she was asking for a little [INAUDIBLE], for things-- some basic things that she couldn't get in Brzeziny. It was Ukraine, in the Ukraine, and we lived at that time for a year. And she was asking from her aunt, Rahel, to send her these basic things that were unavailable in the time of war. And the card arrived. This is the side of the postcard where my mother is writing to ask for help, and what she requests from my aunt, please, to send her.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the brook that was guiding me when I was totally alone the first day-- where to go, which direction? Because this brook told me that it goes-- I knew it goes this way or that way, so I knew my bearings by this brook.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was this brook that I ran away last time I saw my grandmother. It was from this brook that I was following. It was one of the most horrible nights I had, but I was walking near this brook and the trees were guiding me where to go. This is in the same area where I saw my grandmother for the last time, and my sister. But this little bridge-- it's a very tiny, little bridge just to walk over a brook, but it was my guide where I was and which area I was. And when I went back to Poland, it was-- I recognized it because many a time I walked this little bridge over to another area of another village, and I realized where I was. Many a day I walked in this area, hiding and just spending time alone, and just surviving the days. Memories I have. This house is standing on the exact same spot where the house stood that I slept many, many a night in the war. And the woman who lived there had one room, and she gave me a place. She was kind enough. She was a righteous Gentile. She knew I was Jewish and I was alone, and she had three children. And she had no room to sleep. It was a wooden house made like a log cabin. It was log cabin. And it had straw-- at that time, it had a straw roof. Not like now, you see it painted. Because this was the third house that burned down since the one that I was in. But it stood on the same spot, and it's built the same, sideways. And it was in this area that I mostly spent in the woods, at the edge of the woods. And it was this family who lived in this house who helped me the most. If not for them, I don't think I would have survived.
